# PodSecurity `restricted`: Recognizing the Rejection

What the API server error looks like when admission rejects a Pod under
the `restricted` profile, and why the failure surfaces where it does.

> Prescriptive Pod template, five required fields, when-to-apply live in
> [standards/kubernetes/podsecurity-restricted-pod.md](https://github.com/jewzaam/standards/blob/main/kubernetes/podsecurity-restricted-pod.md).

## Recognizing the rejection

The error from the API server lists every violation in one message:

```text
pods "name" is forbidden: violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
  privileged (...),
  allowPrivilegeEscalation != false (...),
  unrestricted capabilities (...),
  runAsNonRoot != true (...),
  seccompProfile (...)
```

Treat the bullet list as a direct checklist against the five required
fields (the standards-side template lists them). Each line maps to one
missing or incorrect field.

## Why this matters

- The rejection happens at admission, so the Pod never starts. The operator
  sees a wall of text and not a familiar `CrashLoopBackOff`.
- Examples copy-pasted from the Kubernetes docs, blog posts, or AI-generated
  snippets routinely omit one or more of the five fields. They work in
  unlabeled namespaces and fail the moment `restricted` is enforced.
- Diagnostic Pods are the place this hurts most — the operator is already
  debugging something else when the admission error appears.
- Chart-managed workloads usually inherit the right defaults from chart
  values; ad-hoc Pods do not. Most charts gate these fields behind a
  `podSecurityContext` / `securityContext` values block.
